nameMeanings.name: Cornelius
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Dutch, German, Danish, and southern English: from the Latin personal name Cornelius (originally an old Roman surname, probably derived from cornu ‘horn’), borne by a 3rd-century pope and Christian saint. In England, this name is rarely found before the 16th century, when the name was brought to England from the Low Countries by Protestants, among whom it was also a popular name in the vernacular form Cornelis. In North America, this surname may also be an altered form of the German or Dutch variant Kornelius.

variants.name: Cornelious
meanings: Americanized form of Dutch, German, or Danish   Cornelius  .
African American: from the personal name Cornelious, from Latin Cornelius .

variants.name: Kes
meanings: Dutch (mainly Edam-Volendam): from a pet form of the personal name Cornelis (see Cornelius ).
Turkish (Keş): nickname or metonymic occupational name from keş, a kind of dairy product.

variants.name: Kess
meanings: German: from a pet form of the personal name   Cornelius  .

variants.name: Koranda
meanings: Czech: from a derivative of the personal name Kornel, from Latin Cornelius , or Konrad .

variants.name: Kees
meanings: Dutch and German: from a vernacular form of the Latin personal name   Cornelius  .
German: variant of   Kase   2 or possibly 1.
English (mainly Essex) and Scottish (mainly Dunbartonshire): variant of Kee , with post-medieval excrescent -s. This surname is rare in Britain.

variants.name: Cornelio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Carlos, Jose, Guadalupe, Mario, Ramon, Adelfo, Agustin, Aniceto, Claudio, Cruz, Damaso, Eleazar.  Italian Antonio, Dante, Bartolo, Carmelo, Carmine, Cesario, Enrico, Filomena, Franco, Luciano, Nicola, Orazio.  Portuguese Joao.
Spanish, Italian, and Portuguese: from the personal name Cornelio, from Latin Cornelius .

variants.name: Cornelison
meanings: Altered form of Dutch Cornelissen: patronymic from the personal name Cornelis + -sen ‘son’. See Cornelius .

variants.name: Corniel
meanings: Dutch: from the French form Corneille of the personal name Cornelius .

variants.name: Corneille
meanings: French and West Indian (mainly Haiti): from the French personal name Corneille, from Latin Cornelius .

variants.name: Cornely
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Chantal, Jean-Paul, Loubert.
German: Latinized (humanistic) patronymic from the personal name Kornelius or Cornelius , based on the Latin genitive Corneli(i).
French (Cornély): from a personal name from Latin Cornelius (see Cornelius ).

nameMeanings.name: Sarah
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Biblical name, borne by the wife of Abraham and mother of Isaac. According to the Book of Genesis, she was originally called Sarai (possibly meaning ‘contentious’ in Hebrew), but had her name changed by God to the more auspicious Sarah ‘princess’ in token of a greater blessing (Genesis 17:15, ‘And God said unto Abraham, As for Sarai thy wife, thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be’). This has been one of the most enduringly popular girls' names.

variants.name: Sara
meanings: Variant of   Sarah .  This is the form used in the Greek of the New Testament (Hebrews 11:11).

historicEvents.description: Thomas Jefferson's American Declaration of Independence endorsed by Congress. Colonies declare independence.
France sells Louisiana territories to U.S.A.

historicEvents.title: Slavery Becomes Illegal in New York State
historicEvents.description: During the years 1799 to 1827, New York went through a period of gradual emancipation. A Gradual Emancipation Law was passed in 1799 which freed slave children born after July 4, 1799. However, they were indentured until 25 years old for women and 28 years old for men. A law passed 1817 which freed slaves born before 1799, yet delayed their emancipation for ten years. All remaining slaves were freed in New York State on July 4, 1827.

en.lifeSummary: Sarah Cornelius was born about 1711, in New York, United States. She married Adam Brass on 6 April 1731, in Hempstead, Nassau, New York, United States. They were the parents of at least 1 daughter.
